days.title: Arrival in Santiago & Maipo Valley Reconnaissance
days.description: Touching down in the forearc basin of the Central Andes. Happy to have arrived safely in South America before heading forth, checked in and ready to explore. Great tour of the town of Santiago. Travelled southeast into the Maipo Valley to observe the transition from the sedimentary valley floor to the igneous Andean foothills. You will be picked up from your hotel to get to know some of the historic sights of Santiago de Chile, such as La Moneda (Presidential Palace) and other places of interest. You will also have time to explore some of the less-known areas of Santiago but equally fascinating and full of history such as the cobblestoned Lastarria Neighbourhood.

images.caption: Figure 1.1: The Santiago basin skyline with the snow-capped Andes rising in the background. A striking picture of earth processes.
Figure 1.2: Close-up of fluvial conglomerate deposits along the Maipo River banks.

geoNote.title: Andean Orogeny
geoNote.text: Plenty of great evidence of rapid tectonic uplift is visible in the deeply incised river valleys. The clasts in the conglomerate are predominantly volcanic, indicating high-energy transport from the volcanic arc. Reminded me of the power of earth processes first learnt at university in Aberystwyth.

days.title: San Cristobal Igneous Complex
days.description: Today you will visit two highlights of central Chile: Villard Winery in the Casablanca Valley and the port city of Valparaíso. Hiked up the hill to observe the intrusive igneous formations overlooking the basin. Casas del Bosque Winery - This vineyard was conceived in 1993 to be a family boutique winery exclusively oriented to the production of high quality wines. It is recognized as one of the most prestigious vineyards within the valley due to the excellence and quality of its wines elaborated following a sustainable philosophy respecting the environment and the local community. Valparaíso - Valparaíso’s picturesque layout, enhanced by the surrounding hills that seem to dive into the ocean, reminds us that this was once the most important maritime destination of the Pacific coast before the Strait of Panama was opened to navigation in 1914. You will visit the downtown area, then discover the colourful hill neighbourhoods. Here, high above the city, you will have fantastic views of the surrounding area. The upper and lower parts of the city are connected with 16 funicular railways built around the turn of the century, when the city experienced tremendous growth. After an amazing experience, you will return to your hotel.

images.caption: Figure 1.1: The Santiago basin skyline with the snow-capped Andes rising in the background.
Figure 1.2: Close-up of fluvial conglomerate deposits along the Maipo River banks.

geoNote.title: Intrusive Rock
geoNote.text: The large hill is largely composed of quartz monzonite, an igneous rock that cooled slowly underground.

days.title: Maipo Valley Strata
days.description: Witnessed intense folding in the sedimentary layers of the Cajón del Maipo. Today you will be picked up early in the morning for a one-of-a-kind tasting in the middle of the desert, with an outstanding view of the Salt Mountain Range. You will then go for a walk to admire the impressive landscapes of the Moon Valley firsthand before continuing to an ayllu, a traditional community. Once there, you will roam its narrow trails surrounded by vegetable gardens and adobe houses. Here you will take a minute to rest and taste typical Atacameñan food. After this wonderful experience learning about the history, culture, and challenges of San Pedro de Atacama, we will call it a day and return to your hotel. You will begin by getting to know two culturally and geologically important sites northwest of San Pedro de Atacama. First, you will visit Yerbas Buenas, an area boasting impressive artistic manifestations including petroglyphs, engravings and paintings. This is the most important display of cave art in the area. Afterwards, you will continue to the Rainbow Valley, where you will learn in plain sight the reason behind its name: thanks to its colourful, million-year-old rocks. At the end of the day, you will gather on the rim of the Salt Mountain Range, above the Mars Valley (also known as Death Valley), where you can contemplate its capricious morphology as well as the beauty of the Andes in the distance.

images.caption: Figure 1.1: The Santiago basin skyline with the snow-capped Andes rising in the background.
Figure 1.2: Close-up of fluvial conglomerate deposits along the Maipo River banks.

geoNote.title: Folding
geoNote.text: The 'V' shaped folds in the canyon walls demonstrate the immense lateral pressure of plate convergence.

days.title: Valparaiso Coastal Uplift
days.description: Explored the UNESCO hills. The steep cliffs show evidence of rapid tectonic coastal rise.

images.caption: Figure 1.1: The Santiago basin skyline with the snow-capped Andes rising in the background.
Figure 1.2: Close-up of fluvial conglomerate deposits along the Maipo River banks.

geoNote.title: Tectonic Uplift
geoNote.text: Major seismic events in this region can uplift the shoreline by over a meter in minutes.

days.title: El Yeso Reservoir
days.description: High altitude reservoir surrounded by turquoise water and gypsum-rich soil.

images.caption: Figure 1.1: The Santiago basin skyline with the snow-capped Andes rising in the background.
Figure 1.2: Close-up of fluvial conglomerate deposits along the Maipo River banks.

geoNote.title: Gypsum Deposits
geoNote.text: The 'Yeso' (Gypsum) found here is an evaporite mineral formed in ancient saline basins.

days.title: Transit to Rapa Nui
days.description: Flight across the Pacific to the most isolated volcanic island in the world.

geoNote.title: Hotspot Volcanism
geoNote.text: Rapa Nui formed from three shield volcanoes emerging from the seafloor over a mantle hotspot.

days.title: Rano Raraku Quarry
days.description: The birthplace of the Moai. Carved directly into the volcanic crater walls.

geoNote.title: Volcanic Tuff
geoNote.text: Tuff is lithified volcanic ash. It is soft enough to carve with basalt tools but hardens over time.

days.title: Orongo & Rano Kau
days.description: The dramatic caldera of Rano Kau shows beautiful freshwater lake stratigraphy.

geoNote.title: Caldera Formation
geoNote.text: A caldera forms when a volcano collapses into itself after emptying its magma chamber.

days.title: Ahu Akivi Caves
days.description: Explored subterranean lava tubes created by ancient flowing magma.

geoNote.title: Lava Tubes
geoNote.text: Caves formed when the outer crust of a lava flow hardened while the molten center kept moving.

days.title: Anakena Beach
days.description: White coral sand contrasting with dark basaltic rocks.

geoNote.title: Coral Sand
geoNote.text: Unlike the volcanic rocks of the island, this sand is biogenic, formed from crushed coral and shells.

days.title: Mainland Return
days.description: Return to Santiago. Evening reflection on the island's volcanic cycle.

images.caption: Figure 12.1: Overview of the Tatio basin showing steam discharge at 06:00.
Figure 12.2: Close-up of siliceous sinter terraces showing active precipitation.

days.title: Patagonia: Punta Arenas
days.description: Flying south to the edge of the continent. Entering the realm of the icy glaciers. Are they retreating?

images.caption: Figure 12.1: Overview of the Tatio basin showing steam discharge at 06:00.
Figure 12.2: Close-up of siliceous sinter terraces showing active precipitation.
Figure 12.3: Map of the South American plate showing the subduction zone and volcanic arc.
Figure 12.4: Geological timeline of the Andean orogeny and volcanic activity.
Figure 12.5: Photo of the volcanic landscape near Punta Arenas with the Strait of Magellan in the background.

geoNote.title: Glacial Moraines
geoNote.text: The landscape here is shaped by glacial till—unsorted sediment dropped by retreating ice sheets.

days.title: Torres del Paine
days.description: First view of the granite massifs. A masterpiece of intrusive geology.

geoNote.title: Laccoliths
geoNote.text: The 'Torres' are granite laccoliths that intruded into sedimentary rock 12 million years ago.

days.title: Grey Glacier Hike
days.description: Walking beside the massive Southern Patagonian Ice Field.

geoNote.title: Glacial Erosion
geoNote.text: Glaciers act like giant sandpaper, grinding down solid rock into fine 'rock flour'.

days.title: The French Valley
days.description: Deep in the heart of the granite horns. Witnessed small snow avalanches.

geoNote.title: Exfoliation
geoNote.text: Granite 'peels' in layers due to pressure release as overlying rock is eroded away.

days.title: Departure Preparations
days.description: Final morning in the park before the journey back to Punta Arenas.

days.title: Atacama Extension: Moon Valley
days.description: Transition to the high desert. Salt crusts crunching underfoot.

geoNote.title: Evaporites
geoNote.text: Halite and Gypsum crystals form here because evaporation rates far exceed precipitation.

glossary.definition: One tectonic plate sliding beneath another.
Rock formed from compacted volcanic ash.
An igneous intrusion that has forced apart two layers of sedimentary rock.
A large volcanic crater formed by a major eruption leading to the collapse of the mouth of the volcano.
